ICAI Convocation · New Delhi · September 2025

Who I am

"I ask why the numbers are what they are — not just whether they balance."

I'm Chetna — a Chartered Accountant based in London. I qualified in September 2025 after three years of practice that took me from statutory audits of small businesses all the way to GST special audits of the State Bank of India.

I moved to London because I wanted to understand the UK profession from the inside — not from a textbook. So I went to Accountex London. I sat in the room at the ICAEW Global Summit. I talked to people. I listened more than I spoke.

I'm someone who finds finance genuinely interesting — not just as a qualification to hold, but as a lens for understanding how businesses actually work. That curiosity is probably the most useful thing I bring anywhere.

My Story

Not a CV.
A journey.

"I didn't just want to pass the exams. I wanted to understand why the profession exists — and what it means to do it well."

01

Where it started — curiosity, not ambition

I didn't choose accounting because it was safe or practical. I chose it because business fascinates me — the way numbers tell the story of every decision a company makes. The CA qualification felt like the most rigorous way to learn that language properly.

Delhi University's B.Com Hons gave me the foundations. But the ICAI articleship — three years of live client work while simultaneously studying for one of the world's hardest professional exams — that's where the real education happened.

02

Three years of real practice

At Vijay Mukesh & Co., I didn't sit in the background. I worked on statutory audits, GST compliance, bank audits, and management reports across sectors I had to quickly understand — from trading companies to major banking institutions.

The highlight was being part of the GST special audit team for the State Bank of India — one of India's largest and most regulated banks. That experience of working under serious regulatory scrutiny, managing huge transaction volumes, and reporting to the Department of Trade & Taxes shaped how I think about audit risk.

03

The decision to come to London

Passing my CA exams in September 2025 was the milestone I'd worked towards for years. But the day after, the question wasn't "what now?" — I already knew. London.

Not because it was the obvious next step for an Indian CA. But because the UK's accounting profession is one I genuinely wanted to be part of. UK GAAP, FRS 102, VAT — a different regulatory framework that would force me to learn again. I wanted that challenge.

I got certified in Xero and UK VAT. I studied IFRS 18 & 15 through Deloitte. And then I showed up — at Accountex, at the ICAEW Global Summit — because I believe the best way to understand a profession is to be in the rooms where it's debated.

04

What I'm building now

I'm looking for an environment where I can do great work — where the audit actually matters, where the team cares about getting it right, and where there's room to grow into a fully rounded UK finance professional.

I don't think of myself as "an Indian CA transitioning to the UK." I think of myself as an accountant who happens to have three years of serious practice and is now building the next chapter here.

How I think

Lessons that actually stuck

Three years of live audits taught me things no exam can. That clients want confidence, not just compliance. That a variance is a story, not just a number. That the most valuable thing an accountant can do is make complexity feel simple.

01

Numbers tell stories

Every variance has a human decision behind it. The skill isn't spotting the error — it's understanding why it happened.

02

Presence matters

Attending Accountex and the ICAEW Summit changed how I understand the UK profession. No article replicates being in the room.

03

Skills have a shelf life

Standards change. Tax rules shift. The accountants who stay relevant keep learning — not because they have to, but because they want to.

04

Communication is the real skill

Audit findings are only useful if they're understood. Writing for non-accountants is harder than passing any technical exam.

05

Adaptability over perfection

Moving from India to the UK's regulatory environment meant letting go of comfort. That discomfort is exactly where growth lives.

06

Patience is professional

Good reconciliations take time. Good careers take time. The same patience I bring to a complex audit is how I build everything.

Tools & frameworks I work with

Moments that shaped me

Showing up in rooms that matter

I believe the best way to understand a profession is to be present in the conversations that define it.

Conference
London, UK

Accountex London

Europe's largest accountancy exhibition. Attending gave me a grounded, practical view of the UK's accounting landscape — from cloud software trends to how UK firms actually think about practice management. No article could replicate being in that room.

UK AccountingIndustry TrendsFintech
Global Summit
London, UK

ICAEW Global Summit

The ICAEW's flagship event — bringing together finance leaders, standard setters, and practitioners from across the world. A rare opportunity to understand how the profession is evolving at the highest level, and to see the values that drive UK accounting up close.

ICAEWGlobal FinanceFuture of Accounting
🏆 1st Place
India · National

Best Paper Presenter — Mega CA Students' Conference

I didn't just attend — I competed. Won first place in the paper presentation category at India's premier CA conference, delivering original research in front of a national audience of finance professionals. Months of preparation, delivered in a single performance.

1st Place · NationalICAI2024
Volunteer
Global

WOFA — World of Finance & Accounting

Volunteered at one of the largest global accounting events, supporting coordination and delegate management for 6,000+ attendees from the international accounting community. Being behind the scenes of an event that scale changed how I understand the profession's global reach.

6,000+ DelegatesGlobalVolunteer

A profession worth showing up for

Every event I've attended has deepened the same belief: finance is built on trust between people — and that trust is earned by showing up, being curious, and doing the work properly.

6K+
Delegates at WOFA

Beyond the qualification

The same patience behind a good reconciliation applies to learning guitar.

I moved to London deliberately — not just for a job, but to build a career in a profession I genuinely care about. Outside of work, I'm someone who thinks carefully and curiously about most things.

Whether that's a chord that doesn't resolve, or a client variance that doesn't quite make sense — the same instinct applies. Sit with it. Find the pattern. Understand it properly.

Let's talk

I'm always open to interesting conversations

Whether you're a recruiter with a role in mind, an accountant who wants to swap notes, or someone who just found this site and wants to say hello — I'd genuinely love to hear from you.

I'm based in London, right to work in the UK, and open to audit and finance roles — on-site or hybrid. But this form isn't just for job enquiries. It's for anyone who wants to connect.
